prompts: Nanomaterials exhibit unique structures at the nanoscale, typically ranging from 1 to 100 nanometers. Their structures can be categorized into zero-dimensional, one-dimensional, two-dimensional, and three-dimensional forms. Zero-dimensional nanomaterials include nanoparticles, like quantum dots, where all dimensions are nanoscale. One-dimensional nanomaterials, such as nanorods or nanotubes, have one larger dimension. Two-dimensional nanostructures, including graphene sheets and nanoplatelets, extend in two dimensions. Three-dimensional nanomaterials, like nanowire arrays or nanoporous materials, form bulk nanostructures with nanoscale features. These structures result in novel properties utilized in electronics, medicine, and materials science.
